text: Half of its silk-lined lid is opened up, but I haven’t approached it yet. A few people speak at the service. Mrs. Eckenrode makes a brief speech about how bright and talented Della was. Gramma sits in the front row and dabs at her eyes with a crumpled tissue. A pastor speaks about rose gardens in heaven, but I tune out the rest of whatever he says. Mom is the last to take the podium, and I have to tune out whatever she’s saying. Her eyes scan the audience, pause on certain people as she speaks. I stare at my lap and let her words become an insect’s drone. Afterward, I see people approach Mom and hug her tightly. I see them nodding, but can’t hear the exchange. I realize I don’t care. The crowd is thinning and I know what I have to do. There’s not a lot of time left, but even so, my mind still teeter-totters on the action. I find myself moving toward the open casket, as if my mind’s already been made up but didn’t bother to tell me. Della wears the dark blue dress she got for Homecoming last year. It’s not exactly boring, but it’s not exciting either. She wears a thin golden necklace with a crucifix, and it makes me roll my eyes. None of us have stepped foot in a church or sang a hymn in over ten years. Even through the layers of make-up someone has caked on her face, I want to describe Della’s expression as peaceful . But peaceful isn’t quite right. I lean down and put my hand against Della’s left cheek. Her skin is cool and feels too smooth and waxy, like a Crayon peeled of its wrapper. With her eyes closed, she looks like I probably do at the bottom of the pool. I move closer, leaning down, and I feel someone stepping beside me, so I straighten up again. Dad doesn’t make eye contact, but his lids are red and raw. All night, he’s been carrying around the same glass with ice and some kind of liquor in it. I’m not sure if he keeps refilling or if he’s simply not drinking it. I know he knows I’m looking at him, but he still doesn’t catch my eyes. His face scrunches up and this high-pitched yip noise erupts from his chest. He shakes his head, clears his throat harshly, and then turns and walks away without a word. I watch as other men speak to him in low voices, nod at his sad face, and clap him on the back or give him bro hugs, even the act of comforting grief is too manly to be tainted by the close contact of a full embrace. I turn back to Della and stare. This is not my sister. This is nothing but a cheap knockoff copy. # I notice the way people look at me now in school. I never used to pay attention, but I see expressions of sympathy on people’s faces. Some are sincere, and some I can tell are sheep going along with the crowd, doing what they think they’re supposed to be doing. But both of the groups make a sad face when they see me and offer the same flat-mouthed chagrin. Then there’s the other people I catch looking. Their eyes tell a story other than sympathy. They give a glance and shift away quickly. Most of them suddenly seem to have forgotten something in their last class. They get away from me as fast as they can, as if they’re scared to catch whatever I have, and one of their siblings will end up dying, too. I have a fleeting feeling I hate all of them, but to be honest, I don’t really feel much at all. I’m half of a void, and I’m reminded of it every day. Della’s empty chair in the classroom. Her empty seat on the school bus. Her now empty, untouched locker. A pair of Della’s sneakers remain in the downstairs hallway for a month after her funeral. One day, Dad came inside and stopped, looked down and stared for a moment, and then walked right back outside and stayed in the garage for a long while. # With Della gone, the house became the empty socket of a pulled tooth. Something used to be there, but it’s gone now, and the only thing you do in your search is probe the spot where it used to be over and over again. Of course, it’s pointless, but that doesn’t prevent you from doing it. At least that’s what the house has become to me . Mom started having friends over. Card game every Thursday afternoon, complete with chardonnay and snack trays. When they see me get home from school and I walk past the opening to the dining room, they smile and go quiet until I reach the top of the stairs. Then they speak in voices too low for me to make out what they’re saying. It’s the tone of voice people talk in when it’s something bad. Part of me wonders if they’re asking Mom how I’m doing, but if I dig a little deeper past that feeling, I find I don’t really care much about that either. Dad is here, but not here. This has changed him, and he’s like a not-quite-in-tune radio station. There’s static all the time, and on some days, there’s more noise than others. I want to grab my father and find the stereo knob I need to adjust and get a clear signal again. But I leave him be. I give a polite if insincere smile to the soccer moms sipping their white wine, and I let Mom do her thing. I go upstairs to our room (yes, I still call it that), and lay down on my bed and my mind explores that empty tooth socket. # It’s Saturday morning and my body isn’t sure
